*[Bug 19060] Fix build on distros that package with --package-metadata*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19060>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
             Status|UNCONFIRMED                 |CLOSED
         Resolution|---                         |FIXED
                 CC|                            |maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch

--- Comment #2 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
Thank you, both (neither has said *how* you replaced `:` - namely by `|`  which
would have been my choice, too.

Fixed now with 89966  (to be back ported soonish).

text:
*[Bug 19060] Fix build on distros that package with --package-metadata*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19060>

--- Comment #3 from Dirk Eddelbuettel (<mailto:edd@debian.org|edd@debian.org>) ---
In the narrow sense 'with emacs', of course. In the wider sense (for testing at
<http://launchpad.net|launchpad.net> for actual ubuntu 26.04 builds) via two files with the patch and
its control file which sit in (source) directory debian/patches/ and hence end
up in the 'debian diff':

<https://salsa.debian.org/edd/r-base/-/blob/master/debian/patches/alter_sed_call>
<https://salsa.debian.org/edd/r-base/-/blob/master/debian/patches/series>

I just made a formal 4.6.0-2 release with it, of course the patch will get
removed once I do the next (patched) upstream release.  Thanks for folding it
in.

text:
*[Bug 19060] Fix build on distros that package with --package-metadata*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19060>

--- Comment #4 from Dirk Eddelbuettel (<mailto:edd@debian.org|edd@debian.org>) ---
BTW the svn commit message is marginally incorrect:

   fix PR#19060; the `:` is newly used in Debian flags

The issue at hand is that _Ubuntu 26.04_ now embeds a payload containing a ':'
(per the bug 'Subject' above) which bites with the ':' separator we use on the
same line. Hence a switch to another separator.  

I am just pointing it out in case you prepare a NEWS entry.

elements.text: [Bug 19060] Fix build on distros that package with --package-metadata

is newly used in Debian flags

The issue at hand is that

now embeds a payload containing a ':'
(per the bug 'Subject' above) which bites with the ':' separator we use on the
same line. Hence a switch to another separator.  

I am just pointing it out in case you prepare a NEWS entry.

text:
*[Bug 19060] Fix build on distros that package with --package-metadata*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19060>

--- Comment #5 from Dirk Eddelbuettel (<mailto:edd@debian.org|edd@debian.org>) ---
For completeness, the generated file `/usr/lib/pkgconfig/libR.pc` then becomes:

```r
> system("cat /usr/lib/pkgconfig/libR.pc")
rhome=/usr/lib/R
rlibdir=${rhome}/lib
rincludedir=/usr/share/R/include

Name: libR
Description: R as a library
Version: 4.6.0
Libs: -Wl,--export-dynamic -fopenmp  -Wl,-Bsymbolic-functions
-Wl,--package-metadata=%7B%22type%22:%22deb%22%2C%22os%22:%22ubuntu%22%2C%22name%22:%22r-base%22%2C%22version%22:%224.6.0-0.2604.2%22%2C%22architecture%22:%22amd64%22%7D
-flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -Wl,-z,relro -L${rlibdir} -lR
Cflags: -I${rincludedir} -I${rincludedir}
Libs.private:
> 
```

Jeroen and I have a vague feeling that this may eventually split beyond Ubuntu
so the patch is a good idea.  Thanks again for including it.

> system("cat /usr/lib/pkgconfig/libR.pc")
rhome=/usr/lib/R
rlibdir=${rhome}/lib
rincludedir=/usr/share/R/include

-Wl,--package-metadata=%7B%22type%22:%22deb%22%2C%22os%22:%22ubuntu%22%2C%22name%22:%22r-base%22%2C%22version%22:%224.6.0-0.2604.2%22%2C%22architecture%22:%22amd64%22%7D
-flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -Wl,-z,relro -L${rlibdir} -lR
Cflags: -I${rincludedir} -I${rincludedir}
Libs.private:
>

Jeroen and I have a vague feeling that this may eventually split beyond Ubuntu
so the patch is a good idea.  Thanks again for including it.

text:
*[Bug 19058] abbreviate() can give unexpected output with non-ASCII input*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19058>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch
             Status|NEW                         |CONFIRMED

--- Comment #2 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
(In reply to Kevin Ushey from comment #1)
> Created attachment 3682
> Use iswlower() rather than islower() when determining if a wide character is
> lowercase in the current locale. (proper SVN patch)

Thank you, Kevin.

Note that  `help(abbreviate)`  does warn about  using it with non-ASCII
characters.  I had hoped that that warning may no longer be necessary, but it
**is**:

The result in this case *depends* on the locale (see below)!
On my platform (Fedora Linux 42,43, or 44),
setting the locale, i.e., the here-relevant `LC_CTYPE`, does work,
and the following code --- my proposed regression test, using your example ---
is revealing:
```
ch1 <- intToUtf8(c(112L, 345L, 237L, 115:116, 345L, 101L, 353L, 101L, 107L))
Sys.getlocale() # just for info ..
for(loc in c("C", "C.UTF-8")) {
    lc <- Sys.setlocale("LC_CTYPE", loc)
    if(!is.null(lc) && nzchar(lc)) { # when Sys.setlocale() worked supposedly
        suppressWarnings(a1 <- abbreviate(ch1)) # FIXME - should it warn even
when "correct"?
        ## In abbreviate("přístřešek") : abbreviate used with non-ASCII chars
        print(a1) # correctly "přst" *in* case
        print(hasUTF8 <- grepl("utf-?8$", print(Sys.getlocale("LC_CTYPE")),
ignore.case=TRUE))
        stopifnot(identical(ch1, names(a1)),
                  identical(c(112L, 345L, if(hasUTF8) 115:116 else c(345L,
353L)),
                            print(utf8ToInt(a1))))
    }
}

```
with the following output (which does *not* render properly in
bugzilla/markdown ...):
```
> Sys.getlocale() # just for info ..
[1]
"LC_CTYPE=C.UTF-8;LC_NUMERIC=C;LC_TIME=en_US.UTF-8;LC_COLLATE=en_US.UTF-8;LC_MONETARY=en_US.UTF-8;LC_MESSAGES=de_CH.UTF-8;LC_PAPER=de_CH.UTF-8;LC_NAME=C;LC_ADDRESS=C;LC_TELEPHONE=C;LC_MEASUREMENT=de_CH.UTF-8;LC_IDENTIFICATION=C"
```
and the interesting one:
```
p<U+0159><U+00ED>st<U+0159>e<U+0161>ek 
   "p<U+0159><U+0159><U+0161>" 
[1] "C"
[1] FALSE
[1] 112 345 345 353
přístřešek 
    "přst" 
[1] "C.UTF-8"
[1] TRUE
[1] 112 345 115 116
```

Can you try the above R code on your two platforms -- after applying the
patch(!!) --
please ?

The exact rendering of the output is of course not the point, but rather that
the assertions
are fulfilled for both locale settings...

<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19058>

--- Comment #3 from Kevin Ushey (<mailto:kevinushey@gmail.com|kevinushey@gmail.com>) ---
Thanks Martin! To confirm, here's what I see on my Linux machine (Ubuntu 24.04)
with the patch applied against r89967.

```
> R.version
               _
platform       aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu
arch           aarch64
os             linux-gnu
system         aarch64, linux-gnu
status         Under development (unstable)
major          4
minor          7.0
year           2026
month          04
day            27
svn rev        89967
language       R
version.string R Under development (unstable) (2026-04-27 r89967)
nickname       Unsuffered Consequences

> ch1 <- intToUtf8(c(112L, 345L, 237L, 115:116, 345L, 101L, 353L, 101L, 107L))
Sys.getlocale() # just for info ..
for(loc in c("C", "C.UTF-8")) {
    lc <- Sys.setlocale("LC_CTYPE", loc)
    if(!is.null(lc) && nzchar(lc)) { # when Sys.setlocale() worked supposedly
        suppressWarnings(a1 <- abbreviate(ch1)) # FIXME - should it warn even
when "correct"?
        ## In abbreviate("přístřešek") : abbreviate used with non-ASCII chars
        print(a1) # correctly "přst" *in* case
        print(hasUTF8 <- grepl("utf-?8$", print(Sys.getlocale("LC_CTYPE")),
ignore.case=TRUE))
        stopifnot(identical(ch1, names(a1)),
                  identical(c(112L, 345L, if(hasUTF8) 115:116 else c(345L,
353L)),
                            print(utf8ToInt(a1))))
    }
}
[1]
"LC_CTYPE=en_US.UTF-8;LC_NUMERIC=C;LC_TIME=en_US.UTF-8;LC_COLLATE=en_US.UTF-8;LC_MONETARY=en_US.UTF-8;LC_MESSAGES=en_US.UTF-8;LC_PAPER=en_US.UTF-8;LC_NAME=C;LC_ADDRESS=C;LC_TELEPHONE=C;LC_MEASUREMENT=en_US.UTF-8;LC_IDENTIFICATION=C"
p<U+0159><U+00ED>st<U+0159>e<U+0161>ek
   "p<U+0159><U+0159><U+0161>"
[1] "C"
[1] FALSE
[1] 112 345 345 353
přístřešek
    "přst"
[1] "C.UTF-8"
[1] TRUE
[1] 112 345 115 116
```

In addition, I now see the expected output for the original case, e.g.

```
> abbreviate("přístřešek")
Warning in abbreviate("přístřešek") :
  abbreviate used with non-ASCII chars
přístřešek
    "přst"
```

(I note the encoding of the characters are mangled in the preview in Bugzilla;
hopefully they survive the actual post...)


The test for me fails with this patch applied on macOS, even though it
succeeded on Linux.

```
> R.version
               _
platform       aarch64-apple-darwin25.4.0
arch           aarch64
os             darwin25.4.0
system         aarch64, darwin25.4.0
status         Under development (unstable)
major          4
minor          7.0
year           2026
month          04
day            27
svn rev        89967
language       R
version.string R Under development (unstable) (2026-04-27 r89967)
nickname       Unsuffered Consequences
> ch1 <- intToUtf8(c(112L, 345L, 237L, 115:116, 345L, 101L, 353L, 101L, 107L))
> Sys.getlocale() # just for info ..
[1] "en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8/C/en_US.UTF-8/en_US.UTF-8"
> for(loc in c("C", "C.UTF-8")) {
+     lc <- Sys.setlocale("LC_CTYPE", loc)
+     if(!is.null(lc) && nzchar(lc)) { # when Sys.setlocale() worked supposedly
+         suppressWarnings(a1 <- abbreviate(ch1)) # FIXME - should it warn even
when "correct"?
+         ## In abbreviate("přístřešek") : abbreviate used with non-ASCII chars
+         print(a1) # correctly "přst" *in* case
+         print(hasUTF8 <- grepl("utf-?8$", print(Sys.getlocale("LC_CTYPE")),
ignore.case=TRUE))
+         stopifnot(identical(ch1, names(a1)),
+                   identical(c(112L, 345L, if(hasUTF8) 115:116 else c(345L,
353L)),
+                             print(utf8ToInt(a1))))
+     }
+ }
p<U+0159><U+00ED>st<U+0159>e<U+0161>ek
"p<U+0159>st"
[1] "C"
[1] FALSE
[1] 112 345 115 116
Error: identical(c(112L, 345L, if (hasUTF8) 115:116 else c(345L, 353L)),  ....
is not TRUE
Execution halted
```

text:
So there seems to be some inconsistent behavior here when working in a C locale
-- however, the test in the UTF-8 locale agrees on both platforms. I'll see if
I can learn more.

So there seems to be some inconsistent behavior here when working in a C locale
-- however, the test in the UTF-8 locale agrees on both platforms. I'll see if
I can learn more.

text:
*[Bug 19058] abbreviate() can give unexpected output with non-ASCII input*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19058>

--- Comment #4 from Kevin Ushey (<mailto:kevinushey@gmail.com|kevinushey@gmail.com>) ---
I think I understand what's happening on macOS. src/main/character.c uses the
rlocale.h header, which introduces R's own locale functions when USE_RI18N_FNS
is defined. IIUC, this happens in <http://configure.ac:2341-2356|configure.ac:2341-2356>, and defaults to on
for macOS and Solaris, and off for other platforms.

Notably, this implies that (in "default" builds of R) functions like
`iswlower()` are going to be locale-aware on Linux, but not on macOS, where
they will behave as though a unicode locale is active regardless of what the
current locale actually is.

elements.text: [Bug 19058] abbreviate() can give unexpected output with non-ASCII input

) ---
I think I understand what's happening on macOS. src/main/character.c uses the
rlocale.h header, which introduces R's own locale functions when USE_RI18N_FNS
is defined. IIUC, this happens in

, and defaults to on
for macOS and Solaris, and off for other platforms.

Notably, this implies that (in "default" builds of R) functions like

are going to be locale-aware on Linux, but not on macOS, where
they will behave as though a unicode locale is active regardless of what the
current locale actually is.
